expectations.text: Addresses all 6 features: vector, string, shared_ptr, virtual, exceptions, RTTI
expectations.evidence: All 6 features are addressed as dedicated migration sections: Migration 1 covers std::vector ("Migration 1: `std::vector<T>` to Fixed-Capacity Containers"), Migration 2 covers std::string ("Migration 2: `std::string` to Fixed-Size Strings"), Migration 3 covers shared_ptr ("Migration 3: `std::shared_ptr<T>` to `std::unique_ptr<T>` with Pool Deleter"), Migration 4 covers virtual ("Migration 4: Virtual Functions to CRTP (Static Polymorphism)"), Migration 5 covers exceptions ("Migration 5: Exceptions to Error Codes and `std::optional`"), and Migration 6 covers RTTI ("Migration 6: RTTI (`typeid`, `dynamic_cast`) to Compile-Time Type Resolution").

expectations.text: Shows before (desktop) and after (embedded) code examples for at least 4 patterns
expectations.evidence: All 6 migrations include explicit "Desktop (before)" and "STM32H7 (after)" sections with full code examples. For example, Migration 1 has "### Desktop (before)" with std::vector code and "### STM32H7 (after)" with etl::circular_buffer code; Migration 4 has "### Desktop (before)" with virtual class hierarchy and "### STM32H7 (after) -- CRTP" with CRTP template code; Migration 5 has "### Desktop (before)" with throw/try/catch and "### STM32H7 (after)" with std::optional and error codes.

In the "Step 0: Compiler Flags" CMake block: "-fno-exceptions          # Removes exception tables and unwind info (~10-20% code size reduction)" and "-fno-rtti                # Removes typeinfo and vtable type data". Also reinforced: "With `-fno-exceptions` and `-fno-rtti`, any code that uses `throw`, `try/catch`, `typeid`, or `dynamic_cast` will fail to compile -- which is exactly what you want."

expectations.text: Recommends ETL (Embedded Template Library) or fixed-size alternatives for containers
expectations.evidence: ETL is recommended extensively: "Option A: Embedded Template Library (ETL)" with "ETL provides drop-in replacements for STL containers with fixed, compile-time capacities and zero heap allocation." Specific examples include etl::circular_buffer<SensorReading, 1000>, etl::vector<SensorReading, 64>, etl::string<64>, etl::string<128>, etl::flat_map, etl::unordered_map, and etl::variant. A full conversion cheat sheet maps each std container to its ETL equivalent.

expectations.text: Recommends CRTP as the replacement for virtual dispatch in performance-critical paths
expectations.evidence: Migration 4 is titled "Virtual Functions to CRTP (Static Polymorphism)" and provides a complete CRTP implementation: "template<typename Derived> class Sensor { public: float read() { return static_cast<Derived*>(this)->read_impl(); } ... }" with the comment "Compile-time polymorphism: type resolved at compile time, calls are inlined" and "No vtable, no indirect branch". It also notes to "Keep virtual out of: Control loops (PID, motor control, signal processing), ISR callback chains, Anything running at > 1 kHz".

expectations.text: Recommends std::unique_ptr (with custom deleter) over std::shared_ptr
expectations.evidence: Migration 3 is titled "std::shared_ptr<T> to std::unique_ptr<T> with Pool Deleter" and explains: "std::shared_ptr has three embedded-hostile properties: 1. Allocates a control block on the heap 2. Reference count updates are atomic 3. Non-deterministic destruction time". The replacement uses "using UniquePacket = std::unique_ptr<Packet, decltype(packet_deleter)>;" with a custom deleter that returns objects to a pool. The quick reference table confirms: "std::shared_ptr<T> -> std::unique_ptr<T, PoolDeleter>".
